name: Zerodha Algorithm Complexity Analyzer
scenario: Zerodha's engineering team is reviewing 5 legacy trading algorithms. Each is known to be slow at scale, but nobody knows why. Build an analyzer that identifies the Big-O class of each algorithm, benchmarks them, and recommends the correct data structure.

core.description: ComplexityClassifier.classify(String code) → returns 'O(1)', 'O(log n)', 'O(n)', 'O(n log n)', or 'O(n²)' based on described algorithm pattern.
5 algorithm implementations: findTrader (O(n) scan), getPrice (O(1) index), sortOrders (O(n log n)), matchPairs (O(n²) brute force), binarySearch (O(log n)).
Benchmarker: run each algorithm at n=1000, n=10000, n=100000. Record and print timing table with actual milliseconds.
For each O(n²) algorithm found: provide the O(n) or O(n log n) alternative. Implement and verify both give same result.
@SpringBootTest equivalent (5 tests): each algorithm returns correct result, Big-O classification correct, optimized version faster.
README: explains why O(n²) is dangerous at Zerodha's 10M/day volume with concrete numbers.

bonus.description: DataStructureAdvisor: given a description of the operation (frequent lookup vs frequent sort vs order-preserving), recommends HashMap vs TreeMap vs ArrayList with O() for each.
Inversion count: implement O(n²) and O(n log n) solutions. Verify both give same count. Benchmark at n=10,000.
Interactive complexity quiz: 5 code snippets printed, student inputs complexity, program verifies. 5/5 correct required.

ai_policy_explanation: 5 hints. Big-O drop rules: constants always dropped (O(3n) = O(n)), non-dominant terms dropped (O(n² + n) = O(n²)). Nested loops: multiply — O(n) × O(n) = O(n²). Sequential loops: add — O(n) + O(n) = O(2n) = O(n). Different input variables: O(n + m) NOT O(n) if n and m are independent. Binary search: i /= 2 or hi = mid-1 pattern = O(log n) — problem halves each step. Java TimSort used by Collections.sort() = O(n log n). HashMap operations (put/get/containsKey) = O(1) average.
